speaker: Rob Moore
words: Ulysses Showalter was raised on the hilly, wooded slopes of Moscow Mountain. His neighbors were mostly southerners, and Ulysses tells the story of one Tennessean who cut his father open in a fight. He also talks about the life and methods of moonshining, Moscow saloons, crooked poker games, and cutting cordwood.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: When I was living down there by the woods, about three miles down, you see, and he went in 10 -cordwood cut. Next thing you know, he had some of the boys had cut him 40 -cord, but he had that just about hauled away, you know, and he wanted a few more cords there. Are you taping this?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Yeah, yeah, we're doing fine

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, yeah, we're doing fine. Oh, well, then if you don't care, that's where it seemed like it was where he was laying it. Yeah, oh no, we're going the other way now. Just to wind it up. Yeah. Yeah, so, Dad's gonna need him to cut it, you know, that 10 -cord. And of course, I was busy digging, but I thought I'd do him all right. So I took my tools up and turned up one morning, you know, and cut a three -cord tree out of a red fir, you know. Of course, you understand, I wasn't a pilot. I just cut that. I cut that tree out of a red fir.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: So the first day I had three -cords, maybe one ten, so the next day I cut another red

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: And I had another one, you know, that was a little bit bigger, you know, that was a So I thought, well, there's a thing here that I've wanted to do all my life that I never got a chance at it, you see. But to see how long it could take me to cut a cord, you know, and all brushed out and ready to saw, you know. Mm hmm. Marked off and everything's ready. So I started sawing and then saw it and split it, you see. So it was big enough, so it was eight cuts and eight cords.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: But my time, I started to work on it and saw it and split it. You know, I had that cord cut in just an hour. So it was split, of course, not filed any time. So I finished the tree on that one. I told Ann, well, I said, we got four -cord and a quarter today, we've got ten -cord and a quarter cut. Mm hmm.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So while we were seeing him last week, I was only saying, you know something?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I said, no. He said you had ten -cord and a half. I said, yeah, I got four -cord and a half. So it was another fellow, Fligger, he was a good cordwood chopper, too. And Fligger, he told me, you know, that he'd cut four and a half, but that's where he got it. See, that had told him that I had, you know, and he didn't want to be left behind. He cut four -cord and a half, as well.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, this was all with a cross -cut saw, too, wasn't it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Cross -cut, yeah. And sledging wedges, you know, and spreader sledging. Of course, it was an extra good day and an extra good timber. See, the three trees made the four -cord and a half. Mm hmm. And two. And then that timer I kept made two -cord and three -quarters of the way. Mm.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Two -cord and a quarter, what was my other show?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, that did make two -cord and a half.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: And a half, huh?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, two -cord and a half. See, the other two made two -cords. And then they cut four -cord and a half, so that hammer I played two -cord and a half.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, did cordwood cutters race against each other very often?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: They owned any tales, you know, and of course, it was like everything you'd ever been around with a soda sack, have you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: On the stationary. The same thing, you know, about everybody, of course, most of them, you know, they always do a little better than the other fella, you know. They get the second chance at it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You know, the other fella tells his story first, right?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: And the fella, he got it on you. Right.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, how about this guy you said, Joe Meeks and Stanley, didn't you kind of race against them?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, they was going to, but Joe Meeks wouldn't race. Of course, if he had it, he didn't want to take his money, but if he had it, I bet you he would have beat him because he really could solve. And where Stanley couldn't, but Stanley could have filed him by far. And so Stanley thought how close he'd come to it when he'd seen me saw him, you know, and found out, you know, I beat him.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I think, I beat a whole minute.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, Stanley thought he was pretty good at one time there, didn't he?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, he was. But, you see, he didn't understand that there was no difference in one man saw him. Well, he was just like a sultan. They saw me over there, you know. He'd come from California and there was a kid there, you know, he was a big husky kid, you know, he'd come along. I was sawing logs, you know. And he went to saw a little bit, see, he was, see, I don't know what the time, it was 19 or 20, and he just took over that story and he was running for all his speed he had, you know, going on and coming. And he said, boy, he said that fella couldn't run a saw like that all day, he sure cut some logs running past his gents. But he never let it go at that, he wanted some more, you know, and was getting his rag on the gas.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: And he mentioned it again, well, I said, do you think you saw him faster than I was?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: When he came up, you know, saw him, I just saw him ride along, but I wasn't speeding, you know, where he stayed going. And he said, no, he said, I was throwing a lot faster than you. I said, no, I said, that's your brother Timus, you know, he was alone back there, you know, had a long way to go up and couldn't solve that. So, I had his brother Timus, you know, and I said, you run it just as fast as you want to. And I was near that fast and I was way ahead of the job. He couldn't believe it, but he showed it to me, and I showed him, you know, that, just about, when you're doubled up on him. And I heard his feelings, but you cried about it.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He said, that ain't according to, what did he call that, the gun? Oh, potters, except for science, you know?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Had books on it. I said, I don't care about it. The book says it or not, I showed you them, and you couldn't believe it. It was so. You see, the way I put the saw through, I let it all hurry down, but I get it full stroke each time and I cut it each time. You see, you've seen them saws, of course, the handle I had was one of the old class saws on the door a little bit, you see. And they used to have them first, they'd go down through the hole here, you know, and saw them. This one here, I took the gun down and framed one of these other handles and passed them down to that, you know. Now I'll be sure, nice grip below the saw level, you know, and then the one on the top. And you see, I'd go down and pull this way, you see. Push that hand and this and pull, and I'd shove it down in there, you know, and then I'd bring it back and be a cut in both ways. Oh, I see, you'd push on the top of the handle and it would push the saw down into it. That and the pull on the bottom of it. And that made us think, boy, put it down, you see. That'll earn Stanley, he couldn't believe it either, but that kid, he was almost sick. He thought you were just faster around it, the faster you cut the gun off. But it wasn't good. It was all the way to your head, and that's where Meeks had him, you see, Meeks would add Stanley. He had it beamed because when the fettest was going on, they'd come in.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, Stanley thought it was all in the filing, didn't he?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, he thought it was all in the filing. And I didn't think that for quite a while, until I got sorry for him, you know, because I knew he was a good father and he knew he was a good father. And he filed in and he thought he'd get it so he'd cut an hill someday, but he never did and then I showed him why.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did you show him that it wasn't all in the filing? Oh, I showed him by beating him, taking his saw, don't you remember?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I told you, he said, at least I can file a saw and cut like you do, your saw does. I said, if you take my saw, I'll take yours. I'll do the same thing with your saw. And you was mine, as I had done with your other saw, and he was mine. And of course when he found out, you see it as something that I thought, no, he knew that he could file that saw, you know. And to have it all cut away from him, why, you know, made him feel bad, you know. And when he found out, because of the pen that saw him, he was just like a kid with a new pup, tickle, tickle me, you know. And I think that was the reason I had to bring my brother over. I saw him a few days after that, come over and had this other tree down, you know, right here, he saw it all, and it was cut, he got a little, and of course you see the other other got a little bit bigger, my brother had a little bit bigger than he did, and he beat him five seconds. And when I saw mine over, bigger than him, I beat my brother 55 seconds, I guess, in the family, and even men. And he said, we never saw a man when he picked him back this tall.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You cut with the back of the saw.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. He figured that would be fair. That would be fair, no. That was quite a thing, I used to enjoy it, about to get up, and then when he, of course the war fun, you know, it felt pretty strong, you see, he had all that timber back in that way, and he was a fellow from Wisconsin, I believe, or Michigan, and he was lining up, you know, as an investment. He'd hire about 500 quarters of wood to cut every winter, you see. That made it kind of nice for the boys around there, you know, to have something to do. But it was nice to get up in the morning, you know, we always got out, got to work by the time we got to see good, and look together, and stand around there, you know, and listen to them wedges, take them, you know, and read your phone, and it was quite a thing. And of course, then we'd go home, probably in the dark, you know, we would go there most of the time, and we'd walk in half an hour, three quarters, seven miles each way, you know, going on the count.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You didn't live in the camps then?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, I had a shack.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: This shack in the area, in Stanley, he got married. He met me when I was 15, I believe. Yeah, he was 32. And we'd just go, you know, we'd write a couple of books. And he was crazy about guns, and so was I, you know. And he got married, and built this house, just out of borging on lumber, you know. It was covered up in flamethrower, you know. And so then we moved over, and there was a farm in the middle, and then they sold it to me for 15, I lived in three minutes, I think.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Sold to you for how much? Huh?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Fifteen dollars.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Fifteen dollars?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. That's pretty cheap. Yeah, pretty cheap. I was too good a house, but you know, he had plenty of wood, and he was a good one. And some of the diamonds, I think it was, come down there, you know, you got a fire in there, you know, you had snow in there, and then down there, freeze it. Down there, you go somewhere, and I think it's gonna be that big, up next to the roof. And probably, I don't know, eight, ten foot long. On the lower side of that had room, you know, to get down before it kicked the ground. Snow. Mm -hmm. It was a hard thing.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, how much would you get for cordwood? Then a cut -and -lift, you mean?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Mm -hmm. Well, most of the time it was a dollar, or a dollar and a quarter, that's when Matt Strong got in there. Of course, he had us when he got in there, I was fifteen, sixteen years old when he first come in. Strong, you know, and bought that. Yeah. And for quite a while, he'd cut for, well, some of them could hire a cut for eighty -five cents a cord, you know. Different colors, I don't know, those cuts, I don't see. A quarter, a quarter.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Huh?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Nothing.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was that the missus?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, newspaper. Oh, newspaper. Thanks. That's all right.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Henry, it's a great little snow -golf, you know, it's part of the field, kind of great for me. Once in a while, you get a mumble bag game, you know. Mm -hmm. You play Mumble-Pig for a game. We're gonna go back to it. You play Mumble-Pig, Henry.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That's where you put knives, isn't it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, and it hasn't been in the game. Usually, I had a knife a little late in it, you see. You start to see. I don't know, I think it was done a different way, so that way you see the wind finger, and then you see the finger, and then flip again. And again, and again. Hold your ear. And change hands. Flip it again. Open your nose and your chin and your mouth and forehead. One thing about that, I don't know how many of you have been diving holes. Uh -huh. Of course, you lost your turn, you know, if you missed a throw. That was probably the next one. Got next, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How would you win a game of Mumble-Pig?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, well, I got through first. Oh. See, you had so many of them things to go through. Uh -huh. And the fellow, you know, every time you missed one, the other fellow took on this, and it didn't become your turn to come around to you again. And the fellow that got out, the old tricks done, and the young fool, he was the one. Uh -huh. Some of them got pretty good at throwing them knives, you know. Pull over here, you know, pull over here and see, I got this over here. and through there, you know, and change it over here again, you know, get this over here on there and throw it the same way, you know. Sometimes, sometimes it wouldn't get very far along, and there was a big one in the middle of the lake. Of course, there's no money that I ever seen put up on it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was there much gambling of other sorts that went on in those camps?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, later on, yeah, they, the loading camps, but not in that wood camp like that, you know, where it wouldn't be so close. It wasn't a loading camp, but they had a big camp, you know, where they'd have a poker game about every night. It'd go nine o 'clock, and quit. That was one thing you could always quit on time, you know, nine o 'clock, which was time to quit. And...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind of things would the early homesteaders do for entertainment?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, there's hardly little to anything that don't, I guess. Some of them are the same. Of course, in the loading camp, they'd be reading a book, some of them playing poker. One thing or the other. And they'd say, well, there's some pretty good singers there, you know, they had a whole bunch of them, some of them Tennesseans and different players, you know. You know, they had a rhythm to their singing, you know. It was pretty nice, some of them. Probably weird it.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: See, them Tennesseans singing on the TV, haven't you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. You know, they got a pretty good rhythm all the time, you know. Mm -hmm. They did pretty good.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: What kind of...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How would you describe the early homesteaders, the first people who came in?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, I don't know, it wasn't much different than anybody else. Only they was not too smart and full of their muscle. Uh -huh. Oh, they got smart, too. I just had that for fun.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, were they...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did they come out here? Why did your father come out here?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, I'll tell you, I think it was because he drank. And his folks were all Christians, you see. And he wanted to get away from them so they'd drink, I guess. That was the only thing he liked, because when he got out here, of course, he had some money, see, when he sold out there, back in Virginia, he was born and raised in Virginia. And he raised tobacco. Mm -hmm. And I don't know how many acres he said he had, but the back of his name, he made good money, and then he sold out to his brother -in -law. He made his own sister. And we'll have somebody come along and build him, and he knew that he could get cheap land in Missouri. And they could raise good tobacco there, too. And he took the word for it, and so we've done all that on time, and of course, they had so many coming every month, it'd be forever. So often, I don't know how many things was, every year, every month, or every what. But anyway, by the way, when he got out there, he found out that he could buy the land cheap, and he raised the back of it with what he called fat. Got it back with him fat. So it didn't settle like it should, you see. So he stayed there long enough, and I had him more than any in Missouri. He sold out and came to Oregon, and he worked at the break yard down here at Athena. Oregon. Oh, it's close there anyway. That's where he got his name.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: At Galena?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Athena. Athena. Oh, uh -huh. I ain't sure just how far it gets from... ...the Philippines. I think it's probably 10 or 15 miles from Pendleton at Athena. He worked at the break yard there at Athena. And that's a little bit of a crime when you go on the site. We had a potato there. He said he used to plan to get off the wheat or something. But he planned to patch the potatoes, you know. He didn't just plow them in. But then he got them covered. And then he said he got the potatoes off of there without any coloration. I weighed four pounds, some of them. Not all of them, you know. Four pounds weight. That's a pretty good spud. Yeah, a big spud, yeah. Of course, I imagine, by the way, if anyone put a four pound inside of a hundred, it wouldn't weigh over two pounds, by the way. That's another two now, back there where Dad's home stay was, you see. Because he got to manure in it. Why, he planted potatoes there. He got to patch and patch potatoes. He'd be in the garden and things like that, you know. And when he got manure...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: To size your thumb?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. And then, he'd be about done with a little bit of a healing. Put your hand in the spoon. And anyway, you know. And after you got the fertilizers, the horse manure was the best fertilizer. I'd raise pretty good potatoes, you know. Cabbage is pretty good. After you got the turpentine out of your drum, you know. Mm -hmm. Was there a lot of drinking and fighting and stuff in the early days with the different... No, no, I'd say later on when the dancers would get fighting on them once in a while, and get a few too many drinks on them, you know. Or somebody'd swipe the other side of the grill and you'd be jealous, you know. And they'd take a fight. Mm -hmm. There were only too many fights. Well, you told me your dad had some trouble one time.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Huh?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: You told me your dad had some trouble one time with that Tennessean. Yeah, Tennessee. That was a lot of school in Tennessee. Tennessee, you see, he'd... What was that dumb dumb thing where they took... You know, they'd get in debt.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: They'd take something, what was that they called that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I'm not sure I know what you mean. I'm not sure I know what you mean. Well, that's... to me, you see, they'd file...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Bankruptcy?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, bankruptcy. Oh. And we'd then have to see this guy.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Your dad had?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, guy had, and then...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: This was Lee Guy?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Dad thought that the fellow, to me, a school trustee, they should be honest, you know. Which is true, too, but some of the guys would argue when they got out of the fight. Of course, Dad, he was built about like I am, you know, as strong as a Knox. The old guy, he was built heavier, but he was strong as a Knox, too. But Dad was much faster with his fists and old guys, so he was a pick of the tool. Guy and old guy, that's it, you know, the fellow was there, he was riding with, you know. He told them, he says, come on, stop, and he says, let's go home. So, correct me, correct me, this old guy got his knife out, and down the wagon, sake. Guy made at him and he hit him, and he said he thought when he hit him, you know, he better not hit him on the head, he might kill him. He had a wife and two children at home, he said, so he hit him on the shoulder and knocked him down. And so, the brother said, well, come on, he says, I thought you were gonna stake your knife and have some at home.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Hi.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Hi.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: So, he was driving, driving a bad team, and he was going in the front of the wagon and taking them back to the hospital. So, he was having a good night, and it was nine o 'clock the next morning, but he got well. He was lucky. Yeah, he sure was.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What were you starting to tell me about the thrashing?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I didn't really know what you were saying.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I had a thrash, you know, I had bunlets, and you never seen any of that, huh?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Seen me, this whole country, all the way around. I'd be in the harvest time, you know, and got ripe enough, and have cut bunlets, you know. I'd be probably, get up in the morning, you know, and stretch. Whistleblower, you know, and there was whistles blowing all over the country, you know. They had a thrash machine, you know, and a little whistle blower, and then to get up, you know. They'd put in about 14 hours a day. All of them had a wagon, you know, with something like a hay rack on it. Wouldn't be such high side, you know. They'd run out that way.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: You'd see them hold a bunch of hay, haven't you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: You'd do that, and you'd go to that place there without having so much of a rack, you know, to pitch over. And they'd go into the machine. The first thing that I ever worked on, I had a place where you'd see the cylinders right here in the machine, you see. I found a very big machine that had a sideboard on each side, you see. And you'd throw the bundle on there, and there'd be a throw there with a, I thought he'd have a sickle blade, you know, and a piece of wood. He'd throw it on there, piece of wood, you know, and a string around his wrist, and he'd go out, cut it, you know, cut the bint, and the string, and then he'd push it, you know, towards the feeder. And he'd have them down, and float down, and he'd reach in there, one hand, one hand, one hand, the other hand. There was a time they'd ban cover on each side. They'd be cutting in bands and shoving them over towards the feeder. The feeder would come in, you know. I guess, you know, you wouldn't believe it, but down here, between here and Poulton, you'd see there was a warehouse there, you'd see what they'd call it. But anyway, there was some Catholics in there. And they'd have, or they'd have a homestead, you see, Divine was their name. And they had only a 27 -inch cylinder, you know, that machine. And it had a, it had a blower on it, you know, it just had a stacker, you know, to go around this way, and turn it, and come back this way, you know, and have slats, you know, just carry the stow out, you know, that had to be all knocked out in the machine. And just keep rotating, you know, and of course the stackers are the ones that they save it while they stack it, you know, so they could turn water pretty good, you know. Otherwise, they wouldn't want to stack it, and call the scene up, but it was a big heavy board, you know, three runners on it, you know.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: And then they'd have a horse on each end of it, you see, two drivers, one on each end. And the horse would bug its way out there, you know, and out of the way.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: And they'd turn their horses, you know, and throw it right out from under it again, and back and get another, well, you see it'd be a pile -up every time it was out, and it was a load, and they'd pile up another load, you see. It was quite a thing, and when they were stacking it over winter's feed, there'd be two on there, the same two, and they wouldn't need the horses. And the old brother in old German there, he was an old time store guy, he had a pipe, you know, and he'd get smoking on there, but he had a cover, you see him, I suppose. And I'd shove it in the smoke on there, you know. I wasn't afraid to say that. Anyway, he'd take it alone, one drip and the next, the other one would take it alone and come back and so forth until I got to the top. And I was going to top it out, you know. Well, that was quite a thing, you know. You get a tail wind sometimes, you know, I got to throwing sacks later on. I swear I'd never thrown out a sack of them, you get a tail wind, you know, and blow that chaff and stuff down your shirt all over again, you know. But by golly, a month before harvest come next year, I'd get the feed.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would you get more money for sowing sacks than you would for hauling bundles?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, oh yeah, I got a lot there, hauling bundles. I got two dollars and a quarter for hauling bundles, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: A day?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, because that's the team he got today. I got the two hundred for him, he got a dollar. I didn't have any, but of course he got all the money anyway. Well, I did get thirty dollars that first year where it was forty -four days and a half, and I did get thirty dollars on them. But anyway, then sowing sacks, I guess the most I ever got was six fifty for sowing sacks, but most of the time it was four dollars. That's quite a bit more. Six fifty, yeah. I got a high temperature fellow, and he didn't want to, he wanted to sweeten stuff, sow sacks, you know. He wanted to dust a pit. So every time he got a chance, you know, he'd give us a bum set so we'd get a tail wind. So this time I said, one day at noon, they went down into a burly, sat me here for a tail wind, I said, no, it's not like that. They had a big journaling going down the pike, and them and the boss, it was real sweet. They had a big laugh, and I got a big laugh. And I was done, I was going to quit. I think we should have worked on it, I mean, four or five hundred dollars. We went over and quit there, and before we got a job again, let's see, hopefully as soon as I went back, I was done what worked for five dollars. And I was down there for six and a half. That old time, the Whitmore, he was the first fellow I ever flashed with, you know. I was down in K -14, you know. I was 15 years old, you know. I was always wearing the scuffle, you know. Because the young man, he was 17, he was on the band cutters. He was jealous of me, I could throw him too. There was one in Sykesville, that was the toughest one I had. He was a little bit too much for them, but I'd get him and bring him to the top of them. The old boss, he wasn't very big, but he was scuffling a little rascal, you know. And boy, he just thought that was swell kids, you know. That fellow, that was 15, he got the best in that. But I thought, well, he done a dirty trick there too.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: You know, that leap frog you know down the hill?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: The sun had gotten rained up in the wrong time, you know, a purpose to throw me on my head. Didn't happen to hurt me, but I held it against him anyway. So the next year, the first time it rained, there was an old shack down there, an old barn, you know. And we all went down there, and the first thing I did was figure out a way out of the way to get him to pull it. And my dad was a rassler, I put him down about six times and he never let me once. I was the last one, he never rassled with me. I don't know how come, it seemed like he'd lost all of his ambition on the rassler. I mean, I was just coming in on mine, you know. Yeah, that's good.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, did people usually rassle instead of fighting with their fists?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, we'd rassle. We wouldn't be a fighting, you know, we'd just be a rassling. To see who could throw the other down. Usually when I start fighting with their fists, they really bring or knock them out every time. Especially a guy like him. He's a pretty powerful hitter.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Me?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, I hit the ground pretty hard. If you wasn't hitting the ground, you could hit with that fist pretty heavy, though. Yeah, I could, but it didn't happen very many nights. I got flicked most of the time. There'd be some barn kid, you know, probably three or four or five years older than me, you know, that wouldn't be any bigger than I was. I'd have more experience and it would back my eyes sometimes. After I got older, I'd have a different thing. The shoe was on the other foot.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You want to tell me some more about, or tell me again about your moonshining days?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, well, let's see now.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, first off, why did you decide to start moonshining? Me?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. Well, I'll tell you, the first moonshine I ever made, I wanted to drink it, you see. Well, it was up here at Moscow. I had a brother down at Palouse. You know where that's at?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And how long had you been around there? Did you know that Ralph Watson lived down there?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Didn't you know Oren Haring, huh?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, anyway, Bill was working down there and him and the little hearing aid was working for this Watson. So, there was a fellow by the name of Bob Nechesny. He was an art attorney with the Haring Pourier. So, he wanted to sell them, you know, he was selling these stills, you see. And one of the neighbors, I mean, was a, you know, a 40 gallon still. And Paul really knew about those things. He could take a little stove, and they could take about three of them and hook them up around the outside. And then they'd burn oil. They'd heat it and have their water, you know, and cold water to run it through. And then the moonshine would come out, you know. So, they made up, I don't know, 25 or 30 gallons before harvest, you know. So, that part of it, they had moonshine oil harvest. We, of course, wouldn't drink them in the winter, you know. It was a time when we'd drink them. Get up in the morning and see the things, not just the food. So, then, in that winter, I was up above, way back up in the mountain, not too far, where I live, and up there there's an old shack up there. So, we was cutting wood for a fellow there, Jack Shope, Sherbuck. And so, we decided we had a little drink, and Brother Heaton found out, you know, to get these, what did they call them, I don't remember what they called them, but they was copper and a whole six -gallon, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Or double boiler?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, it was a single boiler. It singled up. But it got up here, you know, and then it taped it this way. So, you cut a hole there and made it into a little pile of tazita, and then you hang your coal on there, you know, and I would put in your water and get the boil in your water, and then I would try to get that coal water in, and turn in that alcohol, and it would fizz it. So, we'd have a 12 -inch oven and 15 to 20 gallon, it was damp, it wasn't, oh heck, it wasn't that. I couldn't get drunk there, you know. I'm getting up to here. So then, up there, it was a hard time check anyway. The guy said, well, I had to have an oven the same time, you know. And it couldn't touch the wood, and it was completely out, and you know, I couldn't stand it. It was down in the engine, and I was done. So, then we got to making moonshine, and it got well in that. I made a little one -a -two, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So, we went up to Sacheen Lake, I don't know if you've ever been up there, have you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You know where, I think it was up there by Newport, down the lake?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yes. So, it was all through that side of the road there that Sacheen Lake was. Oh, okay. I got to go around there, I was thinking, hmm, I got kind of stuck on the galley, and I guess she kind of fell for me. I was wondering, they do that sometimes. And so, we decided that it would no work, you know. We made a little wood in there, but it didn't become so much fun, couldn't make wood in the winter. So, we moved down here, and I had to pay up on that place, you know, pay the debt to that. So, we got to making moonshine, too, I did, you know. So, it done so well, you know. So, I had to figure out how to, see what old townhouse you might fit around in, but see, this one darn old man, he was jealous of it, you know, he'd sell his moonshine for $4, and I sold mine for $5, and he had his set and come by mine. So, he didn't like that, see, because I made a lot better roads than he did, I had my time to start, you know, and make the right strength, and I double run it. He didn't know what to say about it, and when he put the head in the barrel, he didn't burn the barrel out, you know. He'd pull around the inside, you know, chart, and he'd have to run a few barrels out, you know, and that same barrel, I didn't taste out wood. I'm worried he'd charred the barrel, but he never got any of that to see, it was clear, and he had to pay.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: So, that done pretty well, and that's how it taught me.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You made your moonshine with brand?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, brand instead of grain.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I was trying to see if he'd tell me, he heard me and ran into him, and he said, how are you doing?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I said, I'm pretty well, and he's done.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That was when you got caught, right?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why don't you tell me again about how you would sell it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, I'd just take it in there to my son, and I'd hide it, you know, he had a kind of cubby hole in his house, and I'd stick it in there, you know, and whenever I wanted to sell it on Gallow, you know, or anything, or somebody would come to him, get it, like, get him a gallon, so I'd go over there and get it and take it back to him. That was easy.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And you just sold it by the gallon?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, I did, yeah, he'd find it out for some reason, he didn't take a gallon and sell it back, I'd sell it to him for four dollars, see? To your son? A gallon, yeah, and he'd make ten pints out of it, and sell it for a dollar a pint, you see? Uh huh. He'd make a living that way too. Uh huh.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Here you go.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, when you hauled it to town, would you hide it under a load of hay or something like that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, I'd just hide it in the, I'd hide it, place it right behind you, you know, behind the seat of the scoop, stick it in the trunk, and sometimes right in front, I'd hide it in the clovers or something, you know, between the seats. Uh huh. Well, yeah, that first had the two seats, and I changed it to a coupe, you know, just on the trunk. And we had a cloud of time, there was always a family there, an old man and two boys that always wanted to ride to town with me. Well, well, look at this time. So there, where they, so they had got the goat shoe room where that had been, you know. Turned down south there, you see. They lived down the road there on past the schoolhouse, you know, down there. You know, the fool wanted to catch me, and I come along there, and this time I pulled you boys this time, so I took off there, and I hit the other road over there, and I know, and then I nearly got down there just a time ago, down there. So, gave them a ride.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Didn't want to turn them down, you know, it was good friendly keeping. Uh huh. But I didn't know what to tell them, in case they ever felt any. Just couldn't help it when I had them in the same seats, and discovered everything. Tell them, you know, they're young or something. I guess they were kind of moving.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What about? Well, did you have any close calls with the revenuers before they finally caught you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No. I had some stew pigeons around, and got a sale on me. Uh huh. They were one of my old timers. I was raised right back in there, behind there, and he was, I thought he was alright. And he made me a claim to the girl in the world again, you see. Told me where to take it to go, and didn't. I took it back there, and gave it to him, and he paid me for it. I felt, though, that I was stuck, you know, having the set up. And, well, you see, if I wasn't a father, I'd been raised in that.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: You know Mano Erikson, do you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Uh huh. His cousin, of course, my old Mano, he wouldn't have done anything like that. Yeah. He was a pretty solid guy. But this father was younger. I guess his older brother wouldn't have done it, but this young man, he... Anything he could get a quarter. I guess maybe he got a dollar. Yeah. He probably got the whiskey, you know, and fired up for evidence, you know, if they needed it. I didn't make them furnish my evidence. He didn't give it to the whole thing. He read me my right act, and that was it. But, well, then, probably, you know, I've already appointed him, and he judged me to ask him questions, you see. And then getting down to the right, he made my little lair, you know. So, there was a CC camp over there, you know, and they...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: ...taught him the coffee, you know. Said it right down the center, but then, you know. So, that's all about why... ...the whole work, you know. So, I've known him ever since I was a little kid. And he said, well, you made the mistake. You sold him the CC camp then. I said, heck, I never sold him the CC, see boys.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, why didn't you tell him? I said, did you hear it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He was there, you know. Listen to him, Paul. I said, absolutely. Just ask him questions, why he's done this or done that, and they'd tell him. He'd make them out of the lair. I said, well, you'd have to make me out of the lair, and tell him that. Just tried to get to him, and he didn't even know nothing. Do it. That's how he tried to get to me. Say something, but I didn't say nothing. They'd judge, you know. And tried to get him, and that was all I had to do.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Before prohibition, there were a lot of saloons in Moscow, weren't there?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, before prohibition, there was... ...it was about... I told you there was three on Third Street.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What happened to them after prohibition? The saloons had to close, but where did people go then?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: They were running.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: No, they were running, or where did people go if they wanted to have some good times or have a drink or whatever?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, that's where the moonshine started. You see, they started making moonshines when they had whiskey in them. It used to be, you see, that before prohibition, well, you never hardly ever seen a woman, unless you were a prostitute, take a drink, you know. Or anything, and you never smoked a cigarette. A few months and a while, there was an old lady that would smoke a little corn cob and pipe about three times a day. It was all smoke, and you hardly ever seen it. And then they'd make an old lady, you know, somebody would come along with a bottle and... ...want them to take a drink, you know, something, an old lady would take a little sup, you know, a spoonful. And that's about all the drinking that the women done outside the prostitutes, and... ...so you see there. Very few women done any drinking in the olden times. And then, you see, they got to making the moonshine, and they'd have them in wild parties. You heard about them, maybe you've been to them. I don't think so, no. Well, they'd meet at somebody's house, you know, and get it down the moon. And there'd be two or three women, two or three men, you know, and... ...and around, and dance and sing or anything, you know, and make a party out of it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: But it happened mostly at people's houses, huh?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were there things like speakeasies around here? Like little clubs where you could go?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, once in a while you'd be somewhere in it. But, uh, speakeasies would... ...most of the time, that they'd just go to the houses, you know, and get together and get a gown and a whiskey or a half a gown and... ...a moonshine and have a party, you know. Uh -huh. Drink it. Have a big time, I think. And it was, I enjoyed it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think Moscow was a better town?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Um...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: ...with the saloons or after this, or during prohibition?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: They were better, all the saloons there. Uh -huh. Of course, they'd be out of drink now and then, but you'd get sold in and have the all to be good. Uh -huh. But see, everybody, when they got prohibition, everybody was drinking. You see, you'd take some, say, for instance, you know, like Troy.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, they probably couldn't have the moonshine. They'd be snowing there. They'd go along there, you know, and hide it, you know. The sheriffs and things were looking for moonshine or anything bad. Probably just, they caught you in the bottle while you were in jail. Maybe find you 50 dollars, something like that, maybe more than that. Uh -huh. And, uh, they'd be out of drink. You'd go down the street, you know, on Saturday nights, especially. You'd see a hole right in the front of you. They'd reach in there and get you a bottle, usually. That somebody already beat you to it. And the kids would be, you know, bruised up. So, it was funny, you know, we found our son brother and one old brother. He went to church all the time. Of course, I ain't got nothing yet to church. I'd go check myself and go home to it and breathe in it. But, them days, you know, it was different. And, uh, my brother, he was telling me about snowing up. Another fella tore down a woodshed. And, you know, if they see you take a drink, I'll smell it on you. Why, heck, you was them part, you know. One of the friendly woodsheds, the sheriff, I wasn't afraid of the other sheriffs to give him a drink.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Which sheriff was that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Uh, uh, uh, uh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was it Summerfield?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. So, that was what happened to them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What happened to these guys who were tearing down the woodshed?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, yeah, they found a, found a pint of moonshine, you know, about half full. And he said they were very religious, that's what we're talking about. So, he said they took it, took a drink. And he said, boy, it made them feel good. And, uh, well, after I got it, you know, I got caught in, uh, in the trial. And, uh, I left my guns, you know, they come. So, uh, I was standing on the street after I got out of the penitentiary, you know. He, he was always having big, vivid smiles on his face, you know, up the street, smiling. He, coming up the street there, you know, big smile on his face, and he seen me. I got a little ride -along, but I may never live without that. And, uh, his life seemed like a favorite.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, what was it, ma 'am?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, he's coming alive here. So, his wife, they was, well, I don't know if they had any kids, grown or not, but he was old enough, you know, to have a grown kid. She was a nice old lady, too, you know. And I thought that that old man, you know, the smile, that little smile that he had all night. He was a Christian of it, too, you know.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: But I met her on the street, you know, and she says, hello, Mrs., how are you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: She shut the candle, you know, and talked and talked and talked, and showed me, you know, that it didn't make a better difference than her, what I'd done.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Be nice. Right, right. Showed me a little bit of a Christian interest. Christian, yeah. But, you see, he, if the sheriff would have caught him and taken that drink, but he probably got three months in jail. You know, they don't think about what they do. Right. But the other fellow doesn't. He's a, he's a, he's a, he's a senator and a criminal. Yes, he is.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So, do you think there was less, there was less problem with crime and everything in Moscow when there were saloons and houses and things?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, like there was a lot of them. You know, they'd get full and walk up the streets in a wabaloon and duck into another place. So they thought maybe the sheriff was, the cop was walking by them, you know, and look at Adam and get down off in the street. And of course once in a while they'd get a little bit funny, you know, and think they could defy the law and wouldn't get off. They'd usually get caught up in anything. So John Quist paved their town fine, paying fine.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Paying drunk fines?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: That's what he said. And then he went on with the blues and I don't know whether he quit breaking their mouth, but he died over there at the blues.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He got him a catalogued wife to raise an account with. Pretty nice kids too. And she was awful nice and I think he quit drinking. Of course he left here, you know, and I didn't get him in touch with him, I didn't know whether he quit drinking or not.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was there much problem with, like if, in the saloon days, did the marshal or the sheriff have much problem enforcing the law or did that come in later?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, they never had no trouble at all on the mountain, they didn't. Somebody was out on the street and they were talking about whether the cop would get over them and get over the street. Nothing to it, of course, once in a while they'd pay for them. You know, the plan, they had to have that town paved.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: You know, that was a funny thing, it wasn't very... Once in a while, you know, the first thing they told me was, bully. You know, in the towns, you know, take them all in and clean them, you know, they couldn't pick a fight on them. They had a reputation for going by the scrubber, and the little guy there, he wanted to tie them out and see if they could get a little bit of a scrubber out of them. Some of them were, well, three of them I guess, had come from...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did they come from?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Missouri. Got three brothers. One of them married, he got a homestead, though. He tried to play poker, you know, but these other two, they played poker. And that's why he... He wouldn't mind, but they'd better give it back to them, they wouldn't let him know they were cheating. And Mark Carden, you see, said that if they had told him they were cheating, he'd have told everybody in the country. But when he found them out, he did tell them about it. Well, they... Mark Carden, they... when they ran up against the snake, there was a fellow there, and then a Mick Mick Mick bride. And we had it on them some way, but I always figured out what they told me about this one brother getting out of the gallery. Mark Carden said he could see it coming.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Wouldn't mind, you know, just all the players got to win, too. But anyway, when he found and told everybody, you know, he didn't get anybody to play with them. So they got those, they took the boats and decks, they would swing them out, swing them, drugstorey now. Turned them out and marked them and sealed them up and put them back in there. They paid him for them, you know, and let him settle in and get the price out of them. When he seen one of them, if they win, you know, they got excited. I hate it, we ever came after decks, I'd get one of them. Mark decks, you see. And listen, right, I don't know what, but I figured that the drugstore fellas had a devil's cross then. And told that fellow about it. He'd been playing the Mark Carden, you see. And of course now that's just my guess as to why he beat him. Beat him two lovers, you know, playing Mark Carden.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: And had a sign of stone too, you know, that said, you ever played poker?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, oh yeah. Well, you know, it was a dozen of them. Say, for instance, they was playing away, you know, and you'd see what was coming up, you know, and they weren't going to make something out of it, you know, or something like that. He bet 10 cents for 15, 35 cents for 25, on even numbers, after he won the LFL at a raise, you see. Oh. He bet 35 cents and that the LFL, you know, in the game. Pardon me, at a raise, at the end, you see. He knew twice he was doing anything, you know, giving him a sign of the raise. And of course, they made it bet that. Anyway, this fella, they figured that this fella was so good at it, told me, he said, I don't think you can get that money so hard that I couldn't stand it too much. And of course, they knew probably $100 or $150, $200 in the box, you know. Once, so I figured that was what they'd done, you see, that this drugstore fella had told his big bride. They bought it and let him in on it, you know, and let him take the deck and mark it himself, you know. And he didn't mark it, see all they had was the size, what they marked with, was the size. And the chances are, I figured, that he marked the size and the color and everything. So that would bring quite a advantage to him. And of course, too, there's another thing, he probably could go second or bottom, you know. Pretty good, you know, good thing. Like I say, he broke one up, a guy by the name, went up to Montana. He was playing out there and took it home, stayed up there and raised his feet and everything. Finally, this one fella, there was an old lady up there that ran a restaurant, you know. They called him Molly, you know, she was a regular old lady, you know. So, they had to hold out with this one fella, the children on the other fella, you know. And the other fella knew it, you know, he'd leave the money on the table for the game of play. And when he'd come back, he'd lost it. So he quit him. And so he was telling Molly, he said, I took a new raise and showed you how to gamble. And he done the rest of it, quit me. So, and he was calling her names, you know, this other fella, this other fella. He said he thought about as much of her as he really was doing mother's own. You've seen them kind of old ladies, you know, nice and... You know, he said...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He... She told him. I've seen him jump on to this other fella and then... ...and then he went to her, you know. And so, of course, that fella, he was kind of a scientist, but he didn't do enough. Pardon me, he must have, you know. So, the fella was gonna whip him and...
